sentences.text: Oh, we had friends from our town.
It was-- it was hard.
We didn't have too much food.
We couldn't get too much.
And I met with a-- with friends.
And then, I got typhus.
I was very sick for a few weeks.
And one night-- I don't know if it was true, or if it was dream-- I-- I dreamt that I went up-- up, and my brother opened the door.
And he said, what are you doing here?
Go home.
My mother told them I'm dying already.
I was very sick.
He said tell mother not to cry.
It didn't hurt me much, it's just like a burn with a match.
And he said, and I'm very happy here, but you go home, he said.
And he said, the only thing I was embarrassed when Gentile people buried me.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
I was very sick.
I guess so.
I didn't know what I was doing.
And then, I woke up and I told my mother, you know, I just saw Eliezer.
And she said, don't talk.
Don't talk, you're too weak.
But I said, no, I have to tell you what I saw and what he told.
You shouldn't cry, and that it didn't hurt him.
And he was-- you know, when he opened the door, I saw all the people with beards, religious people.
And they were praying.
And he was like the leader.
And he-- he said he was very happy there.
I told this to my mother.
Very good.
Very good.
He looked happy.
He told me he was happy.
Yeah, he was young.
Sure.
He looked the same like he looked when he was alive.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
This, I don't remember.
I don't know.
I don't remember.
Yes.
Near our house, it was the end of the ghetto.
It went by-- went by us, by our-- by us, around the ghetto.
And
Jewish police, inside.
Outside, I don't know.
We-- we weren't allowed to go outside together.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
No, we did not go out.
Sometimes, they came in.
What they did, I don't remember.
They-- they killed people.
They--
No.
Oh, yes.
The Germans.
They just told people to get out on the-- outside, and they beat them up.
They called them for-- you know, they sent them away to camps, to concentration camps, slowly, before they liquidated the whole ghetto.
It was in April, 1943, when the Warsaw ghetto uprising started, they started shipping out-- taking out the people and sent them to concentration camps.
We had this wall, but we heard all the commotion and the shooting.
They were shooting a lot.
So we went and-- went down to this place, to the-- to the double wall.
It was very tight, and very hot, very miserable, but we had to-- we tried to-- to-- to-- you know, to survive.
And then, when it got quiet, we went out.
And our house was right near the wires, so-- so we went-- we went-- we crossed, and there was a little river, and we crossed the river, and we started walking to our town.
It was quiet-- we walked during the night.
And during the day, we stayed in the woods, in the forest, whatever we can.
What we ate, I don't remember.
And-- and we took a few days, about a week, maybe.
And we came-- because everybody was always promising that they will take-- they will hide us and they will take care of us.
But then, it came-- we went-- my father went-- we went to the-- the different-- you know.
Yeah.
My brother, also-- I forgot to tell you that my brother came to the ghetto.
He was an-- he had a Polish passport.
But he didn't hear-- once in a while, he'd send a letter.
And he gave it to the Jewish police, or to somebody.
[INAUDIBLE] He'd send a letter, not through the post, not-- So-- but he didn't hear from us for a long time.
He was worried.
And he came into the ghetto.
And he knew the policeman.
They were friends-- the Jewish policeman-- so they let him in.
And he came and stayed already with us.
So this brother picked up the wires, and we all crawled under the wire.
And we escaped.
And we tried to walk during the night, because the Germans, you didn't see them during-- they were afraid too.
And nobody saw it.
Nobody saw it.
And it took-- and then, we had not-- we didn't know what to do.
We had no place where to go.
We lost everything.
So we said, we are going back to the ghetto.
So we went back to the ghetto.
It took us-- it took about-- we were-- were wandering around about three weeks, back and forth.
And we came to the ghetto, we didn't have our house.
The ghetto was made smaller, so we didn't have the wall.
And now, months later, was another-- they liquidated again, the other people who were left.
Some people survived the first.
And then-- the first transport.
And then, they told us all to go on the market, all the people.
And everybody-- my brother had this passport, so he tried to put it in a loaf of bread.
And other people put money, this, other things, maybe jewelry, I don't know.
But the Germans took away all the breads, everything what we had.
Nothing.
And they took us to the-- to Majdanek.
Yeah.
Then, took us to the trains.
We walked to the stations.
And they put us in the cattle cars.
And-- and we were talking.
My mother said, how old am I?
I'm 42 years old. 42?
It was 1943.
She was young yet.
And my two brothers were-- well, not two brothers, one, the oldest brother--
Abraham Chaim, the one who had the passport.
And we were talking.
And there was a little window.
Only one little window in this car.
And before the station, the train slows down.
So we-- so my father said, you'll jump, and I'll-- and my brother will jump.
I'll jump after him, and my mother will jump after me.
So my father jumped.
And my cousin was there, Shimon Friedman.
So my father jumped, and my brother.
And his cousin jumped.
And my mother said, [?
Pearl, ?] you jump.
I said, no, I can't.
I'm not going to jump.
Because I was already-- I could not take it, the wandering around and no place where to go.
I said, whatever will happen, will happen.
And my mother didn't jump either.
And my father's sister, with this little boy, was there too.
Sarah Luba, with her little boy-- I don't even remember.
[CLEARS THROAT]
Yes.
My-- my mother's sister, Pearl.
Her name was Pearl.
She was older than me.
They opened the window, they jumped.
It wasn't-- it was big enough to-- to get through.
And the-- the train was going very slow, before every station, slowing down.
No, we couldn't hear, because the-- they were on the roofs marching with the guns, but, I don't know, they-- they were all right.
I-- my cousin told me that my father had a little scratch on the nose.
And they got together.
They-- they-- you know, whoever get-- you know, they got together, walking.
And then, they walked to our town again.
When they came, through the fields, they walked at night.
And during the day, they stayed any place.
So they-- they came-- they came to our town, on a-- to a farm.
And they-- and they knew who it was.
And it was a big barn.
It was a barn there.
And they went in there.
And-- and they stayed during the day.
The owner came.
Victor [?
Manobious ?] was his name.
He came in.
He said my father said to him, Victor, bring me a piece of bread.
He said, OK, at night, I'll bring you a piece of bread.
He came with a gun at night, and he started with my brother.
And my father said, Victor, what are you doing?
What are you doing?
So he killed him, and then he killed my father.
And my cousin-- it was dark-- and my cousin hid in the bushes.
He could not find him.
And when I came home, he told me all about it, how it happened.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And you, meanwhile, were still in the cattle car?
Yes, I was in the cattle car.
And I-- they took us to--
I really don't-- it wasn't so far, maybe a day.
When they opened?
When the cattle car opened, we had to walk like from Lublin.
It stopped in Lublin.
Majdanek is about three kilometers, so we had to walk all this way.
We came on-- came into the concentration camp.
We had to take off-- first, they made a selection.
My mother was holding this little-- my father's nephew, my cousin.
And his mother was holding by the one hand.
So they took them with the children, with all the people.
They were still young, but they took them with them all.
And me, they selected I get to work.
And my mother's sister too came with me.
And then they-- we took off the clothes, and we went to the showers, took off the clothes-- everything.
And they looked us over.
And we took showers.
They gave us clean clothes-- different clothes-- one long-- big shoe, one small shoe, one high heel, one low heel.
And-- and the-- and then, they took us to the barracks.
The barracks, like-- like, you know, like wood-- I-- how do I say it in English?
Like bunk beds.
Bunk beds?
Just boards, one after the other.
A lot.
They were big and a lot of people in each.
There was a [YIDDISH] there.
She took care of the people.
I think they were Jews.
There were-- some were Jews.
In my barrack, not too good, but she had orders.
She had to obey the orders too.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I-- early.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
A what?
A-- yeah.
Sure.
Oh, yes, every morning.
We had to stand there until they counted if everybody's here.
Yeah.
And then, they took us to work.
Well, we put the-- there were stones in one side, like the-- they told us to take it in a different direction.
And then, the next day, they told us to take it someplace else, and then to the same place, back and forth, just to keep us busy.
Well, the Germans.
Oh, yes, a few times.
Shot?
I saw they hung one girl.
They took us all out, the whole camp, from all the barracks, and they were going to hang her.
They said to us, don't say nothing, don't scream, don't yell, because-- and they said that she tried to escape.
And if anybody will try to escape, they'll do the same thing. she-- they left her hanging like this.
She was-- overnight.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was very sad.
Oh, yes, many.
They took them out.
They didn't die inside, they took them to-- they had like a little hospital, infirmary, whatever they called it.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
I don't remember now.
But I know we had the-- on the cloth.
We didn't have our number on the arm, but on the cloth, we had a K L, concentration camp, in German, [? konzentrationslager. ?]
In the morning, we had black coffee.
In the afternoon, they gave us a soup with horse meat.
And in the-- and at night, they gave us a slice of bread.
So we did-- some people couldn't sleep, because they had a piece of bread.
They were hungry.
I used to try to save a little piece for the morning.
And I-- I had-- I ate, and I had mine left for the morning.
I should be, you know, not so hungry.
Oh, yes.
She was already married.
She was [?
Leah, ?] and Pearl, this Pearl [INAUDIBLE], Esther and her sister.
Yeah, I knew a lot of-- [? and Malche. ?]
Well, we-- we always were thinking we'll survive.
And you know, but it's all very sad.
But we saw what's going on.
We talked about home, we had nobody, we were all alone, and what happened to our parents, and this, and that, you know?

All kinds of-- we didn't have much time to talk, because we were working in the fields with the stones, carrying stones and brick, whatever they had there, not just because they needed it, but they wanted us to work for nothing.
And-- and at night, you know, you-- we had to go-- be inside.
We could not get out after the appell.
At night, they also made an appell.
They counted twice a day.
No.
At night?
No.
We weren't allowed.
From the Germans?
No.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Well, they were all cruel, but we tried not to get near them.
We stayed away.
Yeah, they-- they were beating people, the men, mostly.
We saw the men from far-- they had different camps.
They were in different-- men with men, and women with women.
Yeah, I saw them.
They beat a lot of people.
No.
They-- as I told you, they took them to the-- to the hospital, like.
Oh, yes.
Yes, they had selections.
They told us to-- to get out.
And we stayed.
And they-- you know, they-- they-- they looked over the people, they took away some.
All the-- all the people in the-- and, well, whomever they wanted.
I don't know.
They probably took them to other camps, to Auschwitz, maybe.
I don't know, they just took them away.
Or maybe they-- they burned them, maybe to the ovens.
I don't-- we didn't see that.
I-- how could you see it?
I saw the ovens.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
The ovens was like a big oven, and that's all.
I don't know what they-- you know.
[SWALLOWS HARD]
Where?
How I left Majdanek-- I would say about four weeks to a month.
And they registered people to go to the-- I mean, to-- to work to ammunition factories.
But we could not believe them.
But my aunt-- and I went to work, and my aunt was very mad at me.
She said, where did you go today?
They registered people to leave this camp, Majdanek.
I said, you know what?
In the afternoon, I won't go, and I'll register.
When I registered, so she said, what did you do?
You're going to death.
I said, and what are you here?
Not for that?
I figured that I'll jump.
And I knew where my father was supposed to go, to this [INAUDIBLE].
So I said-- she said, because of me, she has to go too.
I said, if you want to go, you go.
If you don't want to go, because of me, you don't have to go, but I'm going.
But then, some-- and then, they selected-- they-- they looked us over.
We had to strip, take off everything.
She did too.
I passed, she didn't.
But then, I came the-- I didn't jump.
They took us to Skarzysko from there.
And how did you go from Majdanek?
They took us in the trains.
Yes.
Always with a cattle car.
I don't remember.
From Majdanek to-- it was-- it was quite a few days without food, without nothing, because it's-- it was far, Lublin from--
It was packed.
I don't know how many, but it was packed.
I don't-- I don't remember that, but it was packed.
Yes.
Yes, we talked.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we still didn't believe-- we didn't know where are we going.
They told us where-- that we going to the ammunition factory, but we-- we didn't know anyway.
But I-- I didn't want to jump.
When the doors opened up, there were police, their police, Germans, and-- and they took us to this camp.
There were three camps, barrack A, barrack B, and barrack C.
A was the best one.
The B was-- I don't know, but-- but the C, they put in the-- the-- in the-- in the-- we made a shelf.
And in the guns, they put in the powder.
Oh, but they-- and the people got so yellow, they didn't have a bed-- a shower [? thing. ?] They used to take us to our camp to get showers, take showers.
They were so yellow, like an egg yolk, from the powder, from the gun powder.
Right.
I was in camp A.
Oh, was a big, big place.
They took us with the-- with guards.
They took us in the morning, and they brought-- and we came back at night, also with guards.
But one week, I-- I worked nights-- they worked 24 hours a day-- and one week, days.
It was different, with smaller barracks.
They were smaller barracks.
And we could go outside and-- outside and talk to friends.
And we're talking about what we talk about what happened to us.
There's nothing else to talk.
We had nobody, no-- they gave us food, a little better than Majdanek, a sweet soup every day.
And the coffee in the morning, the same thing, a piece of bread.
About the same.
And they made selections.
Oh, yes.
They told us to-- I-- to go outside and to line up.
And they looked us over.
Not naked, but like this.
Whoever they didn't like, they-- I don't know where they took them.
And I--
No.
No, no.
First of all, I didn't know everybody.
But from my town, we-- they were with me.
A few people survived.
Slept-- how many girls?
I don't know, maybe-- oh, who knows?
Maybe 50, 60?
I-- I really don't remember how many.
I remember, one woman was pregnant.
And she had a baby on-- on the-- on the bed, on the bunk bed, if you're going to call it a bed.
It wasn't-- and she had a baby, a little boy.
And we were looking.
And she didn't even-- she didn't say nothing.
She had the baby, and they left it.
And it got blue, and it died.
They didn't do nothing to-- (QUIETLY) to him.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS]
Yeah.
Yeah, but the men had separate barracks.
Oh, they worked in the ammunition factories.
Oh, yes.
I was beaten once.
This was the meister, the German.
He wore civilian clothes.
And I-- and, you know, you made the-- the 40 millimeter.
The shells we made, they were so big.
And-- and when they don't come out right, they have a little scratch, they're not good.
But by the time you pick it up-- they wear the hat, and they come out-- and by the time you pick it up, there are a few more [? schmeltz, ?] they used to say, no good.
So he-- he-- he hit me in my face, in the eye, in the face.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Appells?
No, we-- we-- we-- we went outside, because we-- we didn't go by ourselves to the factory, with-- with guards.
I guess it was an appell.
Oy, the winters were bad.
We didn't have much warm clothes, and with no stockings.
Whatever we came from Majdanek, that's all-- that's what we had.
No stockings, no hats, no-- no-- no gloves, nothing.
And we were freezing.
No.
Blankets, they had-- we had.
Oh, I-- I-- I covered myself with a sack from-- from flour they gave us-- you know, from-- from sugar.
I don't know how I'm doing.
Kind of, I'm--
